title: Virtual Music Worlds – Part 4: AI Generated Artists – Music Business Research
text:
Artificial intelligence marks a quantum leap in the creation of virtual artists. Now, not only can music avatars be visually perfected, generative AI can also be used to compose songs and create lyrics. Additionally, tools already exist that can be used to produce realistic music videos. This means that the technological prerequisites for creating successful virtual music stars have been established. Whether these avatars will be able to succeed in the music market, or even enter the international concert market, remains to be seen. In part 4 of the blog series ‘Virtual Music Worlds’, we will examine case studies of AI-generated music avatars developed primarily by large entertainment companies in South Korea and China, to assess their economic potential for the music industry.
Virtual Music Worlds – Part 4: AI Generated Artists
SM Entertainment and aespa
AI-generated artists will also play a role in this. A notable example is the K-pop girl band
aespa
, formed by South Korean company SM Entertainment in 2020. The band consists of real singers performing under the stage names
Karina, Giselle, Winter
and
Ningning
, who have also been given virtual alter egos.
[1]
At the first World Cultural Industry Forum (WCIF), which had to be held online due to COVID-19, SM Entertainment Chairman Soo-Man Lee explained the concept behind
aespa
. The avatars – also known as æ avatars – are not simply representations of the human musicians, but have independent identities that interact in the virtual world: “There is a new concept group with a groundbreaking identity that we can work with. The members of the ‘real world’ and the members of the ‘virtual world’ have AI brains as different creatures, so they can talk to each other, give tides, make friends, and each world. We are planning to show off a completely new concept of storytelling that we couldn’t meet before, such as going back and forth between the worlds by dividing the information.”
[2]
After the four artists were introduced on SM Entertainment’s social media channels between 27 and 30 October 2020,
[3]
their first single, “Black Mamba”, and the music video of the same name were released on 17 November 2020.
[4]
In addition, a short video was posted in which singer
Karina
chats with her avatar,
æ-Karina
, while sitting comfortably on sofas and explains that they also exchange messages via smartphone.
[5]
In October 2021, SM Entertainment released “Savage”, the girl group’s first EP album,
[6]
which immediately stormed to the top of the Korean charts and also caused a stir internationally. This success earned
aespa
a spot on the main stage at the Coachella Music Festival in California. There, the four singers performed three songs alongside their avatars, who were projected onto a screen. Numerous YouTube videos posted by fans show this.
[7]
In 2023, the time was ripe for an international tour by
aespa
entitled “SYNK: HYPER LINE”, with performances by the girl group and their avatars in various countries in Southeast Asia, as well as in the United States and Europe.
[8]
In 2024, the first full-length studio album, “Armageddon”, was released with ten songs, reaching number 2 on the Korean album charts and also appearing on the international long-play charts.
[9]
This was followed by the second live tour – “SYNK: Parallel Line” – which took the girl group to Japan, China, Thailand and Australia in 2024 and continued in North America and Europe in 2025.
[10]
It remains to be seen whether
aespa’s
two-world concept will work. The K-pop girl group functions like a classic music act, with human songwriters and composers writing songs and producing albums. Live performances also follow a traditional pattern, with the æ avatars projected in the background. Only the connection to the 2.7 million fans (as of July 2025) who gather on the company’s own platform Weverse
[11]
also includes a virtual component. However, fans have also criticised the storyline of
aespa
for not always being comprehensible and difficult to convey.
[12]
SM Entertainment will still have to do a lot of creative work to implement the original concept.
Deep Studio Entertainment and Superkind
Despite all the uncertainties, the concept of
aespa
quickly found imitators. In November 2021, South Korean company Deep Studio Entertainment presented the boy band
Superkind
,
[13]
which originally consisted of four real members,
Eugene, Geon, Daemon
and
SiO
, as well as one virtual member,
Saejin
.
Saejin
was modelled on Adamsoft’s first virtual performer,
Adam
, but thanks to AI technology, he could be made much more realistic.
[14]
Unlike to
aespa
, the avatar was an integral part of the boy band and appeared equally with the other band members in the music and promotional videos, as can be seen in the first music video released for the single “Watch Out” in June 2022.
[15]
The five band members performed on a stage in a virtual environment modelled after the Colosseum in Rome, with only the keenest of observers able to tell the difference between the real singers and their virtual band-mates.
Shortly after the release of their debut single in July 2022, another human member,
JDV
, became part of the boy band.
[16]
In December 2022, they were joined by the virtual singer Seung,
[17]
bringing the total number of
Superkind
members to seven.
In March 2023,
Superkind’s
second single, “Moody”, was released, followed by the EP “Profiles of the Future (Λ): 70%” in October of the same year. However, album sales were very modest, with 6,336 units sold in the first week, placing
Superkind
only at number 22 on the South Korean Circle Album Chart.
[18]
After that, things went quiet around the hybrid boy band. Then, on the morning of 11 April 2025, a post appeared on X (formerly Twitter) in which Deep Studio announced the dissolution of the group and the mutual termination of contracts with the five human performers. No reasons were given for this decision, but the AI company thanked the artists for their cooperation and wished them well for the future.
[19]
However, it can be assumed that there were disagreements between the company and the human members of
Superkind
, in which economic factors also played a role. This example shows that AI-generated musicians are no guarantee of success and that the high development costs must first be justified.
Metaverse Entertainment and MAVE
However, South Korea remains fertile ground for the development of AI-generated artists. On 8 November 2022, Metaverse Entertainment, a subsidiary of South Korean game developer Netmarble Corporation, announced the launch of the virtual K-pop girl group
MAVE
for spring 2023.
[20]
In collaboration with the South Korean media group Kakao Entertainment, which provided financial backing for the project and is responsible for music distribution, the first single, “Pandora”, was released on 25 January 2023 as a music video on YouTube. In the video, the four lifelike members of the girl group —
Siu, Zena, Tyra
and
Marty
— perform in a futuristic-looking city and sing about a hopeless future in which emotions have been lost, and about finding hope again in a virtual world called Idypia.
[21]
With the help of an AI voice generator, the virtual singers are able to perform their songs not only in Korean, but also in English, French and Bahasa, the official language of Indonesia. However, the AI voices were trained with human performers and the dance performance in the video is based on motion capture technology, which was already used for
Hatsune Miku
.
[22]
On 30 November 2023,
MAVE’s
second single, entitled “What’s Your Name?”, was released on YouTube.
[23]
Although the debut single had almost 32 million views on YouTube and “What’s Your Name?” was also clicked on almost 12 million times (as of August 2025),
MAVE
failed to enter the South Korean singles charts.
[24]
This could also be the reason why no further songs were released by the AI girl group from 2024 onwards.
Supertone: MIDNATT and SYNDI8
It therefore seems difficult to create a purely virtual AI act if there is no exciting storyline behind it. It is therefore easier to derive an AI avatar from an existing star. This is the case with
MIDNATT
, the alter ego of South Korean superstar Lee Hyun, who formed the vocal group
8Eight
together with Joo Hee and Baek Chan and enjoyed success from 2007 to 2014.
[25]
8Eight
was signed to Korean music company Big Hit Entertainment, which later became HYBE Corporation.
MIDNATT
was developed by South Korean AI company Supertone, which was acquired by HYBE in 2022 for US $32 million.
[26]
In May 2023, the first single ‘Masquerade’ by
MIDNATT
was released as a YouTube video.
[27]
Thanks to Supertone’s voice cloning technology, the song is available in six languages (Korean, English, Spanish, Chinese, Japanese and Vietnamese).
MIDNATT
is the first result of Project L, in which HYBE subsidiary HYBE IM aims to create more AI-generated artists.
[28]
However, the follow-up project came from Supertone, which launched the virtual girl group
SYNDI8
, consisting of the avatars
Canary
,
Nest
,
Goyo
and
Raven
, in July 2024.
SYNDI8
“live” in the virtual
NANSY Land
, whose name is derived from Neural Analysis & Synthesis. The aim is to produce voices as realistically as possible using an AI model. An interesting detail that emerges from Supertone’s announcement about
SYNDI8
is the goal of creating intellectual property for AI-generated voices: “We explore the applications of technology in the music content business and to cultivate own voice intellectual property (IP) to demonstrate the capabilities of creations.”
[29]
Unlike
MIDNATT
and
MAVE
, however,
SYNDI8
are manga-style comic characters, not modelled after humans. The EP “MVP”, released in July 2024, featured music videos produced in comic style for all three songs: “ATOTA”
[30]
, “My Fantasy”
[31]
and “True Colour”
[32]
. Rather than creating hyper-realistic representations of the singers, Supertone focused on generating voices that were as realistic as possible with the help of AI. However, with only a few thousand views between them, the videos prove that
SYNDI8
is an experiment that still needs further development.
Tencent Music and Lucy
Besides the South Korean entertainment companies, the Chinese technology company Tencent is particularly involved in developing AI-generated virtual artists. In November 2022, Tencent Music Entertainment, a Tencent subsidiary, announced that it had produced over 1,000 music pieces using the Lingyin Engine generative AI tool. These have been streamed over 100 million times in China. This technology has also enabled the voices of the late Chinese singers
Teresa Teng
and
Anita Mui
to be recreated.
[33]
Tencent Music went one step further with the
Lucy
project. This is a hyper-real virtual pop star developed by the company’s own Lyra Lab and presented to the public in December 2022 via X (formerly Twitter).
[34]
A month later,
Lucy
already had three songs in the Chinese charts and numerous singles on the Chinese streaming platform QQ Music.
[35]
However, if Tencent Music gets its way, it will not stop at Lucy, but will be joined by other AI-generated music avatars.
The analysis of existing AI-generated music avatars shows that successfully positioning them on the market is difficult. The most likely route to success is to derive AI artists from human musicians, as demonstrated by
aespa
and
MIDNATT
. In these cases, fans have a point of reference in the flesh-and-blood stars, who are perceived as more authentic and thus have greater identification potential. AI artists created from scratch find it much harder to succeed. As well as the considerable development costs, marketing, promotion and distribution costs must be covered for them to become visible on the market. So far, only
Hatsune Miku
has succeeded in building a large, sustainable fan base that attends her concerts. The live music business in particular is the crucial factor in the sustainable establishment of an AI music star. As the example of
ABBA
has already shown, a great deal of technical effort is required to deliver the appropriate live experience for the audience. Simple 2D projections are not enough, and hologram technology has not really established itself on the market. So, to build up an AI-generated music star in a sustainable way and make them successful, in addition to good music, great music videos and exciting social media appearances, what is needed above all is an immersive live experience that will win over the fans. In addition, a
